Sample Digest · Monday 27 April 2026

A recent Neurosurgery digest,
as sent to subscribers

This is the real Monday digest from Monday 27 April 2026. Showing the first 8 of 41 articles from that week — every new paper from the top journals in Neurosurgery.

41articles that week
5journals covered
£0for your first 4 weeks
Note: Summaries are AI-generated from publicly available PubMed abstracts. Always verify against the source article before clinical use.
Acta neurochirurgica

History and evolution of the far-lateral approach in neurosurgery

Hasen MA, de Lotbiniere-Bassett M, Couldwell WT · 2026 Apr 20
Study Type: Narrative review
Key Question: How has the far-lateral approach (FLA) evolved as a surgical technique for accessing the craniovertebral junction and foramen magnum region?
Key Findings:
  • The FLA developed from early limitations of midline posterior and transoral approaches, which caused significant brainstem retraction and high complication rates
  • Technical refinements include consistent C1 posterior arch resection, selective condyle/jugular tubercle drilling, and endoscope-assisted visualization to improve exposure while preserving neurovascular structures
  • Three main anatomical variants (transcondylar, supracondylar, paracondylar) provide modular exposure options tailored to specific lesion locations and pathology types
Clinical Relevance: UK neurosurgeons managing skull base pathology should understand FLA evolution and variants, as this approach offers superior access to ventrolateral craniovertebral junction lesions with lower complication rates than traditional approaches.
Limitations: This is a narrative review without systematic methodology or quantitative analysis of outcomes data.
PubMed →DOI →
Acta neurochirurgica

One-stage hybrid treatment of a ruptured M1 fusiform aneurysm with double-barrel STA-MCA bypass and endovascular embolization

Li Z, Cheng Y, Xu B et al. · 2026 Apr 20
Study Type: Case report/technical note
Key Question: How can ruptured giant fusiform M1 aneurysms be treated when conventional clipping is not feasible due to lack of neck and involvement of perforators?
Key Findings:
  • One-stage hybrid technique combining double-barrel STA-MCA bypass with endovascular coil embolisation and parent artery occlusion
  • Procedure sequence: surgical revascularisation first, then dual-microcatheter coiling of aneurysm and daughter sac, followed by parent vessel occlusion
  • Authors report this approach as safe and effective, with larger calibre STA in adult males enabling high-flow revascularisation
Clinical Relevance: Offers a potential treatment strategy for complex ruptured M1 fusiform aneurysms that are unsuitable for clipping, relevant for UK centres with hybrid neurovascular capabilities.
Limitations: Single case report without outcome data, safety profile, or comparison to alternative treatments.
PubMed →DOI →
Acta neurochirurgica

Trends and disparities in NIHSS reporting and outcomes in acute ischemic stroke hospitalizations: A retrospective cross-sectional study

Malhotra A, Kiss A, Yu H et al. · 2026 Apr 21
Study Type: Retrospective cross-sectional study
Key Question: What are the trends in NIHSS reporting for acute ischemic stroke patients in US hospitals, and how does reporting correlate with patient outcomes and demographics?
Key Findings:
  • NIHSS reporting increased dramatically from 14.33% (December 2016) to 56.27% (December 2022) among 4.5 million stroke hospitalisations
  • After propensity matching, NIHSS reporting was associated with higher routine discharge rates (aOR 1.03, 95% CI 1.01-1.04) and lower mortality (aOR 0.650, 95% CI 0.632-0.668)
  • Significant reporting disparities existed: lower rates in female patients (aOR 0.970), Black patients (aOR 0.963), and Hispanic patients (aOR 0.960)
Clinical Relevance: This highlights potential healthcare inequities in stroke assessment that UK neurosurgery services should monitor, particularly given NHS emphasis on reducing health disparities and standardising stroke care pathways.
Limitations: Cross-sectional design cannot establish causality between NIHSS reporting and improved outcomes, which may reflect unmeasured confounders related to care quality.
PubMed →DOI →
Acta neurochirurgica

The paradox of relief: how decompression sets the stage for hydrocephalus-a retrospective inquiry into its clinical determinants

Daniyal M, Wasi MSI, Davies D et al. · 2026 Apr 22
Study Type: Retrospective cohort study
Key Question: What are the independent risk factors for post-traumatic hydrocephalus following decompressive craniectomy in TBI patients?
Key Findings:
  • Hydrocephalus occurred in 30.3% (43/142) of patients undergoing decompressive craniectomy
  • Two independent predictors identified: lower GCS on arrival (OR 0.88, 95% CI 0.79-0.99) and post-operative interhemispheric hygroma (OR 4.89, 95% CI 1.95-12.28)
  • Predictive model showed strong discrimination (AUC = 0.807) with 81% classification accuracy; surgical technique parameters showed no association with hydrocephalus risk
Clinical Relevance: This provides UK neurosurgeons with evidence-based risk factors to identify TBI patients at highest risk of hydrocephalus post-decompression, potentially informing surveillance protocols and patient counselling.
Limitations: Retrospective design limits causal inference and may introduce selection bias in the cohort analysed.
PubMed →DOI →
Acta neurochirurgica

Early aggressive treatment in WFNS grade v aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage with respiratory or cardiac arrest

Takigami M, Kano H, Bando K et al. · 2026 Apr 23
Study Type: Retrospective cohort study
Key Question: Can patients with WFNS Grade V aneurysmal SAH presenting with respiratory or cardiac arrest achieve meaningful neurological recovery?
Key Findings:
  • Overall favourable outcomes (GOS moderate disability/good recovery) occurred in 14.9% of Grade V patients: 60.0% without arrest, 14.3% with respiratory arrest, and 7.9% with cardiac arrest
  • In cardiac arrest patients, ventricular fibrillation rhythm and shorter CPR duration were significantly associated with favourable outcomes
  • All patients achieving favourable outcomes underwent definitive aneurysm treatment, suggesting benefit from aggressive management in selected cases
Clinical Relevance: This challenges nihilistic approaches to ultra-severe SAH, providing evidence-based criteria to identify cardiac arrest patients who may benefit from aggressive neurosurgical intervention rather than palliation.
Limitations: Single-centre retrospective study with small subgroup numbers limits generalisability of predictive factors.
PubMed →DOI →
Acta neurochirurgica

Creatine kinase level elevation as a marker for motor deficits and symptom duration in lumbar disc herniation patients

Kögl N, Kwan WC, Geiger P et al. · 2026 Apr 23
Study Type: Retrospective analysis of prospective observational cohort
Key Question: Can serum creatine kinase levels serve as a biomarker for motor deficits in lumbar disc herniation patients?
Key Findings:
  • CK levels showed a trend toward elevation in patients with motor deficits (p=0.051) and correlated significantly with MRC grade of the primary affected myotome (p=0.022)
  • CK elevation peaked at 4-7 days symptom duration (ΔCK=317 U/L) compared to 0-3 days (ΔCK=48 U/L), then returned to baseline
  • Higher CK levels were observed when multiple myotomes were affected
Clinical Relevance: This could potentially provide UK neurosurgeons with an objective biochemical marker to quantify motor deficit severity in lumbar disc herniation, complementing clinical examination for surgical decision-making and monitoring.
Limitations: Small sample size (n=87) and borderline statistical significance for the primary outcome limit clinical applicability.
PubMed →DOI →
Acta neurochirurgica

How I do it: Condylar screws

Bolognese PA, Brodbelt A, Foroughi M et al. · 2026 Apr 23
Study Type: Technical note/surgical technique description
Key Question: How should condylar screws be placed as part of craniocervical fusion when occipital bone quality is inadequate?
Key Findings:
  • Entry point is 3mm lateral to foramen magnum with 20° medial angulation toward basion
  • Technique requires sacrifice of posterior condylar emissary vein and continuous hypoglossal nerve EMG monitoring
  • 34mm lag screws provide reliable fixation, particularly beneficial in connective tissue disorders where barplate constructs may be inadequate
Clinical Relevance: This technique expands fixation options for UK neurosurgeons performing craniocervical fusion, particularly relevant given increasing recognition of connective tissue disorders requiring occipitocervical stabilisation.
Limitations: Technical description lacks outcome data or complication rates to assess safety profile.
PubMed →DOI →
Acta neurochirurgica

Superior eyelid lateral transorbital approach for resection of a Meckel's cave schwannoma: how I do it

Emara M, Elhammady MS, El Shammah N et al. · 2026 Apr 24
Study Type: Surgical technique video/case demonstration
Key Question: How can the endoscopic transorbital approach via superior eyelid incision be used to resect Meckel's cave schwannomas with cavernous sinus extension?
Key Findings:
  • Demonstrates feasibility of superior eyelid lateral transorbital approach for accessing Meckel's cave schwannomas extending into cavernous sinus
  • Authors report the technique as safe and effective in selected patients
  • Provides anatomical guidance for this minimally invasive surgical corridor
Clinical Relevance: Offers UK neurosurgeons an alternative minimally invasive technique for challenging skull base schwannomas, potentially reducing morbidity compared to traditional transcranial approaches in appropriately selected cases.
Limitations: Single case demonstration without outcome data, patient selection criteria, or comparative analysis with other surgical approaches.
PubMed →DOI →
